ask user about deal breakers and prefereances. <!----><!----> - use an llm to make a generate a profile. <!----><!----> - make a webpage witha  users full profile, alllowing ueser to see their profile and to edit it. <!----><!----> - webpage  with all rateings and review of books.   <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> vault page: <!----><!----> Donations are exclusive to all mice with a ratio of 1.05 or higher. <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> Every day, you can add up to a maximum of <!----><!----> 2,000 Bonus Points to the VAULT by clicking on <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> and selecting your chosen amount from the dropdown menu. <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> Once the VAULT reaches 20,000,000 bonus points, <!----><!----> everyone who is active on site will get 2 freeleech wedges, <!----><!----> and all those who donated 2k or more will get another 8 freeleech wedge <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> i would like to discuss the three new feature with you <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
This sounds like an excellent expansion of Windlass's capabilities! I love how you are pushing the system from just a passive downloading tool into a fully interactive, highly tuned AI librarian.

To make these new ideas fit perfectly into your existing architecture, I want to validate your approach while gently pointing out a couple of areas where Windlass already handles some of your requests, and where we need to be careful with MAM's rules.

Here is how we can integrate your five new features into the existing windlass-specification.md document.

1. Millionaires Vault Automation

Where it fits: Section 4. MAM Compliance & Torrent Management

You want to automate the 2,000 Bonus Point donation to secure the 10 Freeleech wedges. We can absolutely build this logic, but I must give you a candid warning: MAM strictly forbids automatic scripts in certain contexts, particularly if they resemble spamming or flooding. While Windlass currently performs an "Account Keep-Alive" by hitting the homepage, fully automating vault donations might be flagged by staff as unauthorized botting if they detect perfectly timed, non-human API calls.

To stay true to Windlass's design philosophy of "perfectly emulating a well-behaved MAM user", we should design this as a one-click notification rather than a silent background action.

Proposed Addition to Section 4:

The Vault Guardian: > Windlass monitors the site's Millionaires Vault progress. When a new vault opens, Windlass checks the user's global ratio. If the ratio is ≥ 1.05 (the minimum requirement), Windlass sends a Gotify action-notification: "The Millionaires Vault is open. Click here to donate 2,000 BP and secure 10 Freeleech Wedges." > * Safety First: To strictly comply with MAM's stance against automated scripts, the actual donation requires the user to click the deep-link. Windlass will only prompt the user once per 20,000,000 BP vault cycle.

2. Advanced H&R and qBittorrent Limits Monitoring

Where it fits: Section 4. MAM Compliance & Torrent Management

You mentioned wanting to add H&R monitoring. Windlass actually already has a strict "MAM HnR Compliance Monitor" that forces 100% downloads and enforces a mathematical lock preventing the deletion of any torrent before it reaches 72 hours of seed time. MAM requires every individual downloaded torrent to be seeded back for 72 hours within 30 days.

However, your point about qBittorrent's active/seeding limits is a fantastic catch. If qBittorrent hits its maximum active torrents limit, it might park an unsatisfied torrent, leading to an accidental H&R. We need to expand the existing Unsatisfied Quota Manager to actively orchestrate qBittorrent's queue.

Proposed Modification to Section 4 (Unsatisfied Quota Manager):

Unsatisfied Quota & Queue Limits Manager (Rules 2.5, 2.7, & 2.8): > * Quota Tracking: The core tracks the user's Class Limit for unsatisfied torrents, pausing new downloads if the count approaches the limit (e.g., 50 for User, 100 for Power User).

  • qBittorrent Queue Orchestration: Windlass monitors qBittorrent's global maximum active/seeding limits. To prevent H&R violations caused by the client parking torrents, Windlass actively rotates priority. If the active limit is reached, Windlass will temporarily pause fully satisfied torrents (those with >72 hours seed time) to guarantee that all unsatisfied torrents remain in an "Active/Seeding" state until their 72-hour requirement is met.

3, 4, & 5. The Profile Onboarding Wizard & New Webpage Panels

Where it fits: Section 8. The Control Plane (Web UI)

Your vision for onboarding is much more interactive than the current spec. The current spec mentions a "Short Questionnaire (5 questions max)" and a silent "ABS Library Analysis". We will replace that with your comprehensive wizard and add the new dedicated profile and review pages.

Proposed Replacements & Additions to Section 8:

Onboarding: The Librarian Interview

On first boot, Windlass performs a guided profile initialization:

  1. Library Import & Rating Wizard: Windlass scans the existing Audiobookshelf library. It presents the user with a Tinder-style swipe interface of their existing books. The user can assign a star rating and leave a free-text comment on what they liked or disliked about each specific title.

  2. Dealbreakers & Preferences: The wizard explicitly asks the user for hard boundaries (e.g., "No LitRPG", "Must have a single narrator").

  3. LLM Profile Generation: The LLM ingests all the free-text comments, ratings, and dealbreakers to generate the initial profile_signals database weights.

New Action Center Panels

  • 6. User Profile Dashboard: A dedicated page where the user can view their LLM-generated profile. It translates the raw database weights into plain English. Users can manually edit this page to add or remove preferences at any time without waiting for a post-book debrief.

  • 7. Reading Ledger & Reviews: A historical catalog of every book processed. It displays the data from the reading_ledger and reviews tables. Users can revisit old books, read the free-text reviews they wrote, and retroactively adjust ratings, which will immediately trigger an LLM re-calibration of their profile.

This aligns everything tightly with your existing database structure and UI philosophy.

Given that Windlass is meant to be a "Notification-First" experience where the user rarely opens the web UI, how do you envision balancing the length of the new Onboarding Wizard so that users don't get overwhelmed rating hundreds of books from their existing library?

conversation.question: Can you point me to these rules so i can better understand them? MAM strictly forbids automatic scripts in certain contexts, particularly if they resemble spamming or flooding <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> their LLM-generated profile. It translates the raw database weights into plain English. - i actually want to present the profile as is to users and let them edit it directly.  <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> which will immediately trigger an LLM re-calibration of their profile. I think we ask if they want to re-calibrate <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> Depending on the size of the library we might have to have an llm guide a bit, but if users comes from eg audible the will need to mark books as read to help the what to read next.  <!----><!----> We should also allow users to mark suggested books as already read and give a rating and review <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
Here are the specific MAM rules regarding automatic scripts, along with how we can perfectly incorporate your adjustments to the Windlass profile and onboarding workflows!

1. The MAM Rules on Automatic Scripts

The specific mention of scripts in the site rules is located under the IRC & Shoutbox section, but its underlying principle is enforced site-wide by staff when it comes to site economy and server load:

  • Rule 4.3 Flooding/Spamming: "Flooding/spamming is irritating to other users. This includes, but is not limited to: automatic scripts..."

While the rule explicitly targets chat interfaces, private trackers universally view unauthorized, headless scripts interacting with site economy buttons (like the Vault or Bonus Point exchanges) as "botting" or flooding. Windlass's design already pushes boundaries by hitting the homepage for the Account Keep-Alive (Rule 1.6). By keeping the Vault donation tied to a manual Gotify deep-link click, you avoid generating the perfectly timed, non-human traffic patterns that tracker anti-cheat systems look for.

2. Updating the Spec: User Profile & Re-calibration

I love the idea of exposing the raw profile to the user. It leans into the "power user" philosophy of Windlass.

Revised Web UI Specification (Replaces previous suggestion):

6. User Profile Dashboard: > A dedicated page exposing the core profile_preferences (tag-style rows) and profile_signals (dynamic weights per dimension) exactly as they exist in the database. Users can manually edit these raw key-value pairs or sliders directly.

7. Reading Ledger & Reviews: > A historical catalog displaying data from the reading_ledger and reviews tables. Users can revisit old books, read their free-text reviews, and retroactively adjust ratings.

  • Optional Re-calibration: When a user edits a past review or rating, Windlass does not automatically overwrite the profile. Instead, it presents a prompt: "Do you want to re-calibrate your AI profile based on these changes?" This puts the user in total control of their LLM weights.

3. Updating the Spec: Audible Imports & "Already Read" Suggestions

You are absolutely right that users migrating from outside ecosystems need a frictionless way to establish their history without having to "download" a book they already know.

Revised Action Center & Suggested Next Listens Specification:

The "Already Read" Workflow: Users can quickly build their reading_ledger bypassing the download queue entirely:

  • Universal Input Box: If a user pastes an Audible or Goodreads URL for a book they have already finished, they can click an "Already Read" toggle.

  • Suggested Next Listens: Every AI-curated card in this panel now features an "Already Read" action button alongside Approve, Reject, and Snooze.

  • Immediate Capture: Clicking "Already Read" instantly opens a modal prompting the user for a star rating and a free-text review. This data is injected directly into the reading_ledger and reviews tables, and the LLM uses it to immediately refine the user_profile weights for future recommendations.

When a user marks a suggested book as "Already Read," how detailed do you want that review modal to be—should it just be a quick star rating and an optional text box, or do you want to optionally trigger the conversational "Interview" style if they rate it poorly?

conversation.question: I would like to standadize to one review style that is use for all reviews.  <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c1874527609="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> I do not mind the notification to donate to the pot. That is a reasonable way to do it. <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
That makes perfect sense. Standardizing the review interface keeps the user experience clean and predictable, whether they are importing an old favorite or dropping a book halfway through.

I am glad the notification approach for the Vault works for you! It strikes the perfect balance between taking advantage of the site economy and staying safely within MAM's rules.

Here is how we can unify the review system in the specification, streamlining the "Post-Listening & Recovery" section.

Unifying the Review System

We will replace the distinct "DNF Autopsy" and "Post-Book Debrief" concepts with a single, powerful standard component.

Proposed Update to Section 7.3 & Section 8:

The Universal Review Component

To ensure a consistent user experience, Windlass uses a single, standardized review interface across all interactions. Whether the user finishes a book, triggers a DNF, completes the onboarding wizard, or marks a suggested book as "Already Read," they are presented with the same UI card.

The Interface:

  • Star Rating: 1 to 5 stars.

  • Free-Text Box: A single open text field prompted with, "What did you think?" (or "What went wrong?" if triggered by a DNF).

The Pipeline:

  1. The user submits the standardized review.

  2. The raw text and rating are saved permanently to the reviews and reading_ledger tables.

  3. The LLM ingests the review text, extracts the underlying sentiment (e.g., parsing "Just a pacing slog, no real plot" or "Loved the witty banter"), and automatically updates the dynamic weights in the profile_signals table.

  4. If the review was generated from an existing library import or "Already Read" tag, no further action is needed. If it was triggered by a completed or DNF'd book, the system seamlessly transitions into the "what's next" suggestion or the Bailout Protocol.

By standardizing this, the LLM backend always expects the same data payload format, making your Rust Functional Core much easier to maintain.

Since we are unifying the review interface, should this standard review card also include a simple checkbox or toggle to explicitly "Add to Dealbreakers/Must-Haves," so users can enforce a hard rule immediately without having to navigate to their raw profile page?

conversation.question: Can you five me the new sections so i can paste them in to the document? <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
Here are the exact sections formatted in Markdown, ready for you to copy and paste directly into your windlass-specification.md document.

Replace/Update in Section 4: MAM Compliance & Torrent Management

Markdown
- **Unsatisfied Quota & Queue Limits Manager (Rule 2.8):** The core tracks the user's Class Limit for unsatisfied torrents (e.g., 50 for User, 100 for Power User). It continuously polls qBittorrent for torrents that have *not yet* reached 72 hours of seed time. If the active unsatisfied count approaches the limit, all new automated downloads are paused. Furthermore, Windlass monitors qBittorrent's global maximum active, downloading, and seeding limits. To prevent H&R violations caused by qBittorrent parking torrents when limits are reached, Windlass actively orchestrates the queue, temporarily pausing fully satisfied torrents to guarantee unsatisfied torrents remain actively seeding.
- **MAM HnR Compliance Monitor (Rules 2.5 & 2.7):**
  - *No Partials:* Forces qBittorrent to download 100% of torrent contents.
  - *HnR Lock:* Auto-eviction is mathematically prohibited from deleting any torrent that has downloaded data until `seed_time ≥ 72 hours`.
  - *Safe Deletion:* Stalled or dead torrents are only automatically deleted and blacklisted if they have downloaded exactly 0 bytes.
- **The Vault Guardian:** Windlass monitors the MAM Millionaires Vault. When a new vault cycle reaches 20,000,000 BP, Windlass checks if the user's global ratio is ≥ 1.05. If eligible, it fires a Gotify notification: *"The Millionaires Vault is open. Click here to donate 2,000 BP and secure your Freeleech Wedges."* To strictly comply with MAM's rules against automated scripts, the system will never execute the donation via headless background scripts; it requires the user's explicit click via the notification deep-link.

Replace inside Section 7.3: Post-Listening & Recovery

(Remove the previous "The DNF Autopsy Interview" and "The Post-Book Debrief" subsections and replace them with this single unified component)

Markdown
#### The Universal Review Component
> A standardized, single-interface review system used across all interactions to ensure a consistent user experience and clean data ingestion.

**Execution:** Whether a user finishes a book, triggers a DNF, completes the onboarding wizard, or marks a suggested book as "Already Read," they are presented with the exact same UI card. 
- **The Interface:** A standard 1 to 5 star rating scale, accompanied by a single free-text box prompted with *"What did you think?"* (or *"What went wrong?"* during a Bailout).
- **The Pipeline:** The raw text and rating are saved permanently to the `reviews` and `reading_ledger` tables. The LLM ingests this payload, extracts the underlying sentiment, and automatically adjusts the dynamic weights in the `profile_signals` table. For finished or DNF'd books, submitting the review seamlessly transitions the user to the "what's next" suggestion or Bailout protocol.

Replace/Update in Section 8: The Control Plane (Web UI)

(Replace the existing Onboarding section, update 1. Suggested Next Listens, and add panels 6 and 7 to the Action Center list)

Markdown
### Onboarding: The Librarian Interview
On first boot, Windlass performs a guided profile initialization:
1. **Library Import & Rating Wizard:** Windlass scans the existing Audiobookshelf library. It presents the user with a UI to quickly process their existing books. The user can assign a star rating and leave a free-text review (using the Universal Review Component) for any title they wish.
2. **Dealbreakers & Preferences:** The wizard explicitly asks the user for hard boundaries, dealbreakers, and core preferences (e.g., "No LitRPG", "Must have a single narrator").
3. **LLM Profile Generation:** The LLM ingests all the free-text comments, ratings, and dealbreakers to generate the initial `profile_signals` database weights.

### Action Center

*(...existing intro text...)*

#### 1. Suggested Next Listens
AI-curated recommendations based on `user_profile`, reading history, and current mood signals. 
- **The "Already Read" Workflow:** To easily build history without downloading known books, every AI-curated card features an **"Already Read"** action button alongside *Approve*, *Reject*, and *Snooze*. Furthermore, users can paste an external URL (e.g., Audible) into the Universal Input Box and tag it as "Already Read".
- **Immediate Capture:** Clicking "Already Read" instantly opens the Universal Review Component. The rating and text are injected directly into the `reading_ledger`, and the LLM uses it to immediately refine the user's profile.

*(...existing panels 2 through 5...)*

#### 6. User Profile Dashboard
A dedicated control panel exposing the user's LLM profile exactly as it exists in the database. It displays the core `profile_preferences` (tag-style rows) and `profile_signals` (dynamic weights per dimension). Users can manually edit these raw key-value pairs or sliders directly. This ensures the user is always in total control of the AI's logic, without relying exclusively on inferred data.

#### 7. Reading Ledger & Reviews
A historical, searchable catalog displaying all data from the `reading_ledger` and `reviews` tables. 
- Users can revisit old books, read their past free-text reviews, and retroactively adjust ratings.
- **Optional Re-calibration:** When a user edits a past review or rating, Windlass does *not* automatically overwrite the profile. Instead, it presents a prompt: *"Do you want to re-calibrate your AI profile based on these changes?"*

conversation.question: regarding section 4 should we have a feature for reading and changeing qbittorrent settings? both for recommended clients and for how many torrents windlass have to work with. if qbt is set something dumb eg 1 total torrent then what? <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
That is an excellent catch. If qBittorrent is bottlenecked by its own settings, Windlass’s intelligent queueing math completely falls apart. You cannot orchestrate a symphony if the instruments are locked in their cases!

We should absolutely have Windlass actively monitor and, where necessary, auto-tune qBittorrent’s internal configuration. This also gives us a perfect opportunity to programmatically enforce MAM’s strict technical uploading and downloading rules.

Here is how we can integrate this into Section 4. MAM Compliance & Torrent Management.

Proposed Addition to Section 4

Markdown
- **qBittorrent Configuration Validator & Auto-Tuner:** Windlass does not just send torrents to the client; it actively manages the client's internal configuration via the WebAPI to ensure optimal throughput and strict tracker compliance.
  - *Queue Bottleneck Detection:* Windlass reads qBittorrent's `max_active_downloads`, `max_active_uploads`, and `max_active_torrents` settings. If the user sets these limits artificially low (e.g., limiting total active torrents to 5 when the user's MAM unsatisfied limit is 50), Windlass calculates the mathematical bottleneck. It then sends a high-priority Gotify alert explaining the issue and offering a one-click "Auto-Tune qBittorrent" action to optimize the queue limits for current pipeline depth.
  - *Privacy & Tracker Enforcement (Rule 6.1):* Windlass continuously monitors qBittorrent's network settings to ensure DHT (Distributed Hash Table), PeX (Peer Exchange), and Local Peer Discovery are strictly disabled globally, as requiring DHT to be disabled is a core rule for private trackers. If a user accidentally enables them, Windlass silently auto-corrects the setting and logs the intervention.
  - *Client Version Watchdog (Rule 2.3):* While Windlass cannot change the Docker container's image version, it polls qBittorrent's build version and cross-references it with MAM's "Recommended and Approved Clients" list. If an unlisted, alpha, or beta version is detected, it flags an "Unauthorized Client" warning in the UI.

This ensures that Windlass is never fighting against a misconfigured download client, and it keeps the user perfectly safe from accidental tracker leaks or version bans.

If a user explicitly tries to override an auto-tuned setting in qBittorrent directly (like turning DHT back on), should Windlass aggressively revert it every time it polls, or should it just throw an alert and pause operations until the user fixes it?
